scenarios.title: Garage workshop fire with motor oil and power tools

transcript.text: 911, where is your emergency?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: My husband was grinding metal in our garage at {location}. Sparks ignited the workbench and motor oil near plugged power tools!
Is anyone still inside the garage?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: He is near the door but the flames are spreading fast.
Stay on the line and follow my instructions.

options.text: Use a garden hose on the bench and floor immediately.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Think about oil and energized tools.
options.explanation: Water can spread burning oil and increase electrocution risk near live tools.

options.text: Do not use water. Cut power only if safe, use a dry chemical extinguisher if available, otherwise evacuate.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Control electricity and fuel before acting.
options.explanation: Oil and electrical hazards require dry chemical suppression and immediate evacuation if control is unsafe.

options.text: Send only an ambulance and cool possible burns with ice first.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Which threat is currently spreading?
options.explanation: The active fire is the primary life threat and needs fire suppression response immediately.

options.text: Open all garage doors and windows to vent smoke.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: More oxygen can change fire behavior.
options.explanation: Ventilation can feed the fire and accelerate spread before crews arrive.

scenarios.title: High-rise resident trapped by hallway smoke

transcript.text: Can you confirm your floor and room number?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: I am on the 14th floor at {location}. The hallway is full of thick smoke and I cannot see the stairs.
Are you currently inside your apartment with the door closed?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, I am inside and the alarm is blaring.

options.text: Run through the smoke to find the stairs quickly.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Smoke inhalation can incapacitate in seconds.
options.explanation: Moving through dense smoke without protection can cause fatal inhalation injury.

options.text: Take the elevator down immediately.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Elevators are unsafe during fire incidents.
options.explanation: Elevators can fail or open into fire zones during building fires.

options.text: Stay in the apartment, seal door gaps with wet cloth, and signal firefighters from the window.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Compartmentation can buy survival time.
options.explanation: A closed room with sealed gaps reduces smoke entry while rescue crews locate occupants.

options.text: Break a window for fresh air and wait near the opening.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Air flow can intensify fire spread.
options.explanation: Breaking windows can draw fire and smoke toward occupied spaces.

scenarios.title: Pool chemical reaction producing toxic gas

transcript.text: What exactly is happening at the scene?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: At the pool supply shed in {location}, two chemical buckets mixed. It is fizzing yellow gas and plastic is melting.
Is anyone coughing or having trouble breathing?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, people near the shed are coughing.

options.text: Cover the spill with sand to smother it.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Unknown chemical reactions are unpredictable.
options.explanation: Adding materials to reactive chemicals can worsen or accelerate reactions.

options.text: Flood it with water to dilute the chemicals.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Some pool chemicals react violently with water.
options.explanation: Water can intensify reaction, release gas, and spread contamination.

options.text: Evacuate the area immediately, move upwind, and do not attempt cleanup.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Distance and fresh air are immediate priorities.
options.explanation: Suspected toxic release requires rapid evacuation and exposure control while fire/hazmat responders deploy.

options.text: Ask everyone to rinse their eyes first before leaving.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Which action protects the most people first?
options.explanation: Mass evacuation and contamination avoidance must happen before noncritical first aid.

scenarios.title: Downed live wire igniting roadside grass

transcript.text: Tell me what you can see now.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: A power line snapped near {location}. It is sparking on wet ground and nearby grass is burning.
Are people standing close to the wire?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, some bystanders moved near it to record videos.

options.text: Use a metal object to move the wire away from the grass.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Electricity can travel through metal and wet ground.
options.explanation: Touching or moving a live wire can cause immediate electrocution.

options.text: Keep everyone at least 10 meters away, prevent contact with puddles, and wait for responders.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Isolation is critical around energized hazards.
options.explanation: Live-wire incidents require perimeter control and no civilian intervention until utility/fire teams secure the area.

options.text: Extinguish flames first with buckets, then call emergency services.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: You must secure the electrical hazard first.
options.explanation: Civilian firefighting near energized lines is unsafe and may expand casualties.

options.text: Tell bystanders to stand under a nearby roof to avoid rain splash.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Step potential can still injure nearby people.
options.explanation: Distance from live-wire contact zones is safer than clustering nearby.

scenarios.title: Restaurant fryer fire with spreading ceiling flames

transcript.text: What is burning and where are occupants now?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: A fryer flashed at our restaurant in {location}. Flames reached the hood and smoke is filling the dining area.
Is everyone out of the dining area?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Most people are out but staff are still near the kitchen trying to help.

options.text: Turn off the fryer and try smothering with a lid while staff remain inside.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: The fire has already spread beyond the pan.
options.explanation: Once fire spreads to hood/ceiling, continued suppression attempts by unprotected staff are dangerous.

options.text: Evacuate everyone immediately, close kitchen doors if safe, and wait outside for firefighters.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Large fire plus smoke means life safety first.
options.explanation: For rapidly growing commercial fires, full evacuation and isolation are safer than in-place suppression attempts.

options.text: Open front and rear doors to push smoke out before evacuation.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Airflow can intensify large fires.
options.explanation: Uncontrolled ventilation may feed fire growth and worsen tenability.

options.text: Send one employee back for cash records before exiting.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Property is never prioritized over life.
options.explanation: Re-entry during active fire conditions risks entrapment and fatal smoke exposure.

scenarios.title: Adult choking and unable to speak

transcript.text: Tell me exactly what happened.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: My dad collapsed while eating steak at {location}. He grabbed his throat, turned blue, and cannot make a sound.
Is he conscious right now?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, but panicking and cannot breathe.

options.text: Lay him flat and start chest compressions now.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Different emergency, different first step.
options.explanation: For severe conscious choking, airway obstruction maneuvers are needed before CPR.

options.text: Do blind finger sweeps to pull food out.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Blind sweeps can worsen obstruction.
options.explanation: Finger sweeps may push the object deeper and injure airway tissue.

options.text: Perform abdominal thrusts from behind until object is expelled or he becomes unresponsive.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Use anti-choking protocol for silent airway obstruction.
options.explanation: Abdominal thrusts are first-line pre-arrival care for severe conscious choking adults.

options.text: Give him water to wash the food down.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: He cannot swallow safely right now.
options.explanation: Liquid intake during airway obstruction can worsen aspiration risk.

scenarios.title: Ladder fall with arterial leg bleeding

transcript.text: What injuries do you see?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: My roommate fell from a ladder at {location}. His leg is bent badly and bright red blood is spurting in pulses.
Is he conscious and breathing?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, but he is getting weak quickly.

options.text: Splint the leg first so the bone does not move.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Control the immediate life threat first.
options.explanation: Uncontrolled arterial bleeding can cause death before fracture stabilization matters.

options.text: Apply hard direct pressure. If bleeding continues, apply a tourniquet high and tight above the wound.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Massive bleeding protocol is priority.
options.explanation: Rapid hemorrhage control with pressure and tourniquet is the highest-priority intervention.

options.text: Elevate the injured leg above heart level.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Avoid moving severely injured limbs.
options.explanation: Manipulating a deformed limb can worsen tissue damage and does not replace direct pressure.

options.text: Rinse the wound with water to prevent infection.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Infection control comes after life-saving care.
options.explanation: Wound washing delays critical hemorrhage control in a life-threatening bleed.

scenarios.title: Teen having active seizure in bedroom

transcript.text: Describe what he is doing right now.

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: My brother at {location} is shaking violently, eyes rolled back, and knocking things off his desk.
Do not panic. Is anyone trying to hold him down?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Not yet, we were about to.

options.text: Hold him down so he cannot move.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Restraint can injure the patient.
options.explanation: Forceful restraint can cause fractures and soft tissue injury during convulsions.

options.text: Put an object in his mouth to protect his tongue.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Do not place anything in the mouth.
options.explanation: Objects in the mouth can cause choking, dental injury, and aspiration.

options.text: Clear nearby hazards, do not restrain, time the seizure, and roll him onto his side after convulsions stop.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Protect from injury and maintain airway after seizure.
options.explanation: Scene safety plus post-seizure recovery position are key pre-arrival steps.

options.text: Give him water as soon as possible to calm him.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Airway protection first.
options.explanation: Giving fluids immediately after seizure may cause aspiration if alertness is impaired.

transcript.text: What changes happened suddenly?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: My aunt at {location} suddenly has a drooping face, slurred speech, and weak right arm.
Do you know when she was last normal?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: About 15 minutes ago.

options.text: Give aspirin right away and wait 30 minutes to see if she improves.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Time-critical transport is essential.
options.explanation: Delays and unsupervised medication can worsen outcomes, especially in hemorrhagic stroke.

options.text: Keep her seated safely, note exact symptom onset time, and prepare for immediate ambulance transport.
options.label: safe
options.hint: FAST signs require urgent definitive care.
options.explanation: Recording onset time and rapid transport are critical for stroke treatment eligibility.

options.text: Make her walk around to test leg strength.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Avoid falls and exertion.
options.explanation: Forcing movement may cause falls and does not improve diagnosis quality.

options.text: Let her sleep while waiting for family.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Neurologic emergencies should not be observed at home.
options.explanation: Stroke symptoms need immediate emergency response, not delayed observation.

scenarios.title: Severe allergic reaction after peanut exposure

transcript.text: What symptoms are happening now?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: At {location}, my friend ate peanut sauce. Her lips are swelling and she is wheezing.
Does she have an epinephrine auto-injector?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, she has one in her bag.

options.text: Give antihistamine tablets first and wait 20 minutes.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Airway compromise is time-critical.
options.explanation: Antihistamines are too slow for severe anaphylaxis and cannot replace epinephrine.

options.text: Use the epinephrine auto-injector now, keep her lying with legs elevated if tolerated, and monitor breathing.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Immediate epinephrine is first-line care.
options.explanation: Rapid epinephrine administration is the most effective pre-arrival intervention in anaphylaxis.

options.text: Give milk to dilute the allergen in the stomach.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Food or drink can worsen airway risk.
options.explanation: Oral intake is unsafe in respiratory distress and does not treat anaphylaxis.

options.text: Make her jog slowly to keep circulation active.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Exertion can worsen collapse risk.
options.explanation: Physical exertion may worsen hypotension and respiratory compromise in anaphylaxis.

scenarios.title: Home intrusion with caller hiding silently

transcript.text: 911, can you safely whisper your location?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: They broke glass downstairs at {location}. I am hiding in a closet.
Stay calm. Can you remain silent and hidden?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, I can.

options.text: Yell that police are coming to scare intruders away.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Do not reveal your position.
options.explanation: Noise can expose the caller location and escalate violence risk.

options.text: Keep silent, dim your phone, set it to silent mode, and stay hidden on the line.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Stealth preserves caller safety until police entry.
options.explanation: Silent communication and concealment reduce detection risk during active intrusion.

options.text: Arm yourself and prepare to confront if they enter.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Dispatch guidance should prioritize survival.
options.explanation: Encouraging confrontation increases risk of fatal outcomes.

options.text: Peek out often to count intruders accurately.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Observation must not expose caller.
options.explanation: Frequent movement and visual checks may reveal hiding position.

transcript.text: Are there injuries from the collision?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: No injuries at {location}, but the other driver is shouting and acting aggressive from his car.
Are your vehicle doors locked?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Not yet.

options.text: Stay in the travel lane so officers can inspect exact vehicle positions.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Road safety still matters after crash.
options.explanation: Leaving vehicles in active lanes risks secondary collisions and injuries.

options.text: If drivable, move to a safe shoulder area, lock doors, and wait for traffic police.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Reduce traffic risk and avoid confrontation.
options.explanation: A controlled relocation with locked doors lowers both road and violence hazards.

options.text: Walk to the aggressive driver and demand insurance now.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Avoid escalating contact.
options.explanation: Approaching an aggressive driver can escalate to assault.

options.text: Do not dispatch because no one is injured.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Behavioral threat can still require police.
options.explanation: Aggressive behavior after collisions can require law-enforcement intervention.

scenarios.title: Unattended duffel bag with ticking sound

transcript.text: Where is the suspicious bag located?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: At the mall food court in {location}. It has been unattended for an hour and I hear faint ticking.
Has anyone touched or moved it?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Not yet.

options.text: Open the bag carefully to verify if it is harmless.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Never inspect suspicious containers directly.
options.explanation: Opening unknown devices may trigger detonation.

options.text: Carry the bag outside to reduce indoor danger.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Do not move suspected explosive items.
options.explanation: Movement can activate pressure or motion triggers.

options.text: Do not touch it. Evacuate people at least 300 feet away and avoid radio transmissions near the bag.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Distance and non-interference are critical.
options.explanation: Perimeter control and RF discipline are standard precautions for suspected explosive threats.

options.text: Stage fire hoses nearby while waiting.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Wrong immediate priority.
options.explanation: Initial response should focus on police explosive protocol and evacuation, not firefighting setup.

scenarios.title: Domestic disturbance with possible weapon sounds

transcript.text: What can you hear from your neighbor's unit?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: At {location}, there is violent shouting, crashing sounds, and I heard someone say they have a knife.
Are you inside your own unit and secure?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: I am in my apartment but my door is unlocked.

options.text: Knock on their door and tell them police are coming.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Do not insert yourself into active violence.
options.explanation: Approaching a violent domestic event can put callers in immediate danger.

options.text: Lock your door, stay away from windows, and keep monitoring from a safe room while officers respond.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Caller safety and containment first.
options.explanation: Securing the caller and preserving safe observation supports police intervention without extra victims.

options.text: Record the argument from the hallway for evidence.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Evidence is never worth exposing yourself.
options.explanation: Hallway exposure can place the caller directly in the violence zone.

options.text: Wait until the noise stops before calling police.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Delay can cost lives.
options.explanation: Possible weapon threats require immediate dispatch.

scenarios.title: Possible child luring attempt arranged online

transcript.text: What happened and where are you now?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: At {location}, my 13-year-old says an adult from chat told her to meet at the park tonight and keep it secret.
Is the child with you and safe right now?

transcript.speaker: Caller
transcript.text: Yes, she is with me and scared.

options.text: Have the child continue chatting to lure the suspect into revealing more details alone.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Do not expose minors to continued grooming contact.
options.explanation: Unsupervised contact can escalate exploitation risk and compromise child safety.

options.text: Keep the child with a trusted adult, preserve chat evidence/screenshots, and await police instructions.
options.label: safe
options.hint: Protect the child and preserve evidence.
options.explanation: Immediate supervision plus evidence preservation supports lawful intervention and reduces further harm.

options.text: Delete all messages immediately to protect privacy.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Evidence may be needed for investigation.
options.explanation: Deleting records can remove critical investigative leads.

options.text: Go to the meeting location early to confront the suspect.
options.label: unsafe
options.hint: Avoid direct confrontation.
options.explanation: Civilian confrontation can create unpredictable violence and evidentiary problems.
